q: Which of the following correctly lists all the phases of a compiler in order?
options: A) Lexical Analysis â†’ Syntax Analysis â†’ Semantic Analysis â†’ Intermediate Code Generation â†’ Code Optimization â†’ Code Generation
B) Syntax Analysis â†’ Lexical Analysis â†’ Semantic Analysis â†’ Code Generation â†’ Code Optimization â†’ Intermediate Code Generation
C) Lexical Analysis â†’ Semantic Analysis â†’ Syntax Analysis â†’ Code Optimization â†’ Intermediate Code Generation â†’ Code Generation
D) Lexical Analysis â†’ Syntax Analysis â†’ Code Generation â†’ Semantic Analysis â†’ Code Optimization â†’ Intermediate Code Generation
explanation: A compiler processes source code through six ordered phases: (1) Lexical Analysis â€“ breaks source into tokens; (2) Syntax Analysis (Parsing) â€“ checks grammatical structure; (3) Semantic Analysis â€“ checks meaning/type correctness; (4) Intermediate Code Generation â€“ produces machine-independent IR; (5) Code Optimization â€“ improves IR for efficiency; (6) Code Generation â€“ produces target machine code.

q: The primary function of the lexical analysis phase of a compiler is to:
options: A) Check whether the program is grammatically correct
B) Read the source program character by character and produce tokens
explanation: Lexical analysis (scanning) is the first phase of a compiler. It reads source code character by character, groups characters into meaningful sequences called lexemes, and outputs tokens (categorized lexemes). For example, the lexeme 'int' produces the token KEYWORD.

q: What is the difference between a lexeme and a token in compiler design?
options: A) A token is the actual string in the source code; a lexeme is its category
B) A lexeme is the actual character sequence in the source; a token is the abstract category it belongs to
C) A lexeme and a token are the same thing
D) A lexeme is produced by the parser; a token is produced by the semantic analyzer
explanation: A lexeme is the actual string of characters matched in the source code (e.g., 'while', '123', 'myVar'). A token is the symbolic name representing the category of the lexeme (e.g., KEYWORD, INTEGER_LITERAL, IDENTIFIER). The lexer produces (token, lexeme) pairs.

q: Which of the following is NOT a valid token category in most programming languages?
explanation: Comments are stripped out during lexical analysis and are not passed to the parser as tokens. Valid token categories include identifiers, keywords, literals (integer, float, string), operators, punctuation/separators, and special symbols.

q: A 'pattern' in lexical analysis refers to:
options: A) The sequence of characters forming a specific lexeme
B) A rule (often a regular expression) describing the set of lexemes that form a token class
C) The data structure used to store tokens
D) The parse tree produced by the parser
explanation: A pattern is a ruleâ€”usually expressed as a regular expressionâ€”that describes which strings belong to a particular token class. For example, the pattern [a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9]* describes valid identifiers. When the lexer matches a lexeme against a pattern, it produces the corresponding token.

q: Which tool is commonly used to automatically generate a lexical analyzer from a set of regular expression rules?
explanation: LEX (or its GNU equivalent Flex) is a lexical analyzer generator. It takes as input a specification file containing regular expression patterns and associated actions, and generates C code for a lexical analyzer (scanner). YACC/Bison are parser generators, and GDB is a debugger.

q: What does an interpreter do differently from a compiler?
options: A) An interpreter translates the entire source program to machine code before execution; a compiler executes line by line
B) An interpreter translates and executes the source program statement by statement; a compiler translates the entire program first, producing an executable
C) An interpreter only handles high-level languages; a compiler handles low-level languages
D) An interpreter uses a symbol table; a compiler does not
explanation: A compiler translates the entire source program into target code (machine code or object code) before execution. An interpreter translates and executes the program one statement at a time without producing a separate executable. Interpreters are generally slower for repeated execution but allow interactive use. Python (CPython) is an example of an interpreter.

q: An assembler is responsible for:
options: A) Converting high-level language code to machine code directly
B) Converting assembly language mnemonics into machine language (binary/object code)
C) Linking multiple object files into a single executable
D) Loading a program into memory for execution
explanation: An assembler converts assembly language programs (using mnemonics like MOV, ADD, JMP) into machine language (binary object code). It operates one level below a compiler. The compiler may generate assembly code, which the assembler then converts to object code.

q: The linker in the compilation process is responsible for:
options: A) Converting source code into assembly language
B) Combining multiple object files and resolving external references to produce a single executable
C) Loading the executable program into main memory
D) Performing lexical analysis on the source code
explanation: The linker takes one or more object files produced by the compiler/assembler and combines them into a single executable program. It resolves external references (e.g., calls to library functions), merges code and data sections, and assigns final addresses. Static linking includes library code in the executable; dynamic linking references shared libraries at runtime.

q: What is the role of a loader?
options: A) It translates high-level language into machine code
B) It resolves symbolic references between object files
C) It loads the executable program from secondary storage into main memory and initiates its execution
D) It optimizes the intermediate code produced by the compiler
explanation: A loader is a system program that loads an executable file from disk into main memory (RAM) and transfers control to it to begin execution. It may also perform relocation (adjusting addresses) and dynamic linking. The sequence is: Compiler â†’ Assembler â†’ Linker â†’ Loader â†’ Execution.

q: Which phase of the compiler is responsible for checking type compatibility and undeclared variables?
explanation: Semantic analysis checks the meaning of the program. It verifies type compatibility (e.g., you cannot assign a string to an integer variable), checks that all variables are declared before use, validates function argument counts and types, and ensures control flow constructs are used correctly. It uses the symbol table extensively.

q: Intermediate code generation produces code that is:
options: A) Directly executable on the target machine
B) Written in assembly language specific to the target processor
C) Machine-independent and easier to optimize before final code generation
D) The final output of the compiler
explanation: Intermediate code (e.g., three-address code, quadruples, triples, or SSA form) is a machine-independent representation of the source program. By inserting this phase between semantic analysis and code generation, the compiler can apply machine-independent optimizations and retarget the backend for different architectures without changing the front end.

q: Which of the following is an example of three-address code (a common intermediate representation)?
options: B) t1 = z * w; t2 = y + t1; x = t2
C) PUSH y; PUSH z; MUL; PUSH w; ADD; POP x
explanation: Three-address code (TAC) uses instructions of the form 'result = operand1 op operand2', where each instruction has at most three addresses/operands. Complex expressions are broken into a sequence of simple assignments with temporary variables. Option B correctly shows x = y + z*w decomposed into TAC. Options C and D represent stack-machine code and abstract syntax tree notation respectively.

q: In the context of regular expressions used in lexical analysis, what does the operator '*' (Kleene star) signify?
options: A) One or more occurrences of the preceding element
B) Zero or more occurrences of the preceding element
C) Zero or one occurrence of the preceding element
D) Exactly two occurrences of the preceding element
explanation: The Kleene star (*) in regular expressions denotes zero or more repetitions of the preceding element. For example, a* matches the empty string, 'a', 'aa', 'aaa', etc. The '+' operator means one or more, and '?' means zero or one. These are fundamental operators in defining token patterns.

q: The regular expression (a|b)*abb represents:
options: A) All strings over {a,b} ending in 'abb'
B) All strings over {a,b} starting with 'abb'
C) All strings over {a,b} containing exactly one 'abb'
explanation: (a|b)* matches any string (including empty) over the alphabet {a,b}. Followed by 'abb', the full expression matches all strings over {a,b} that end with the suffix 'abb'. Examples: 'abb', 'aabb', 'babb', 'ababb'. This is a classic example in automata theory used to illustrate NFA/DFA construction.

q: A Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA) differs from a Non-deterministic Finite Automaton (NFA) in that:
options: A) A DFA can have Îµ-transitions (epsilon transitions); an NFA cannot
B) For each state and input symbol, a DFA has exactly one transition; an NFA may have zero, one, or multiple transitions
C) A DFA accepts a larger class of languages than an NFA
D) An NFA always has fewer states than the equivalent DFA
explanation: In a DFA, for every state and every input symbol, there is exactly one transition (the transition function is total and deterministic). In an NFA, a state may have zero, multiple, or epsilon (Îµ) transitions for the same input symbol. Both DFA and NFA recognize exactly the same class of languages (regular languages). The DFA may have exponentially more states than the NFA in the worst case.

q: What is an Îµ-transition (epsilon transition) in an NFA?
options: A) A transition that consumes the current input symbol
B) A transition that moves from one state to another without consuming any input symbol
C) A transition that resets the NFA to its start state
D) A transition that occurs only on the end-of-input marker
explanation: An Îµ-transition (epsilon transition) allows an NFA to move from one state to another spontaneously, without reading (consuming) any input character. Epsilon transitions make it easier to construct NFAs from regular expressions (e.g., using Thompson's construction), but they must be eliminated when converting to a DFA.

q: The subset construction algorithm (also called the powerset construction) is used to:
options: A) Convert a DFA into an equivalent NFA
B) Convert an NFA (possibly with Îµ-transitions) into an equivalent DFA
C) Minimize the number of states in a DFA
D) Convert a regular expression directly into a DFA
explanation: The subset construction (powerset construction) algorithm converts an NFA into an equivalent DFA. Each state in the resulting DFA corresponds to a subset of NFA states. Starting from the Îµ-closure of the NFA's start state, for each DFA state and input symbol, the algorithm computes the Îµ-closure of the set of NFA states reachable. The DFA may have up to 2^n states for an NFA with n states.

q: The Îµ-closure of a state q in an NFA is defined as:
options: A) The set of all states reachable from q by consuming exactly one input symbol
B) The set of all states reachable from q by following zero or more Îµ-transitions (including q itself)
C) The set of all states that have a transition to q on some input
D) The set of all accepting states in the NFA
explanation: The Îµ-closure of state q is the set of all NFA states reachable from q by following zero or more Îµ-transitions, always including q itself. It is computed using a graph reachability algorithm (BFS/DFS on Îµ-edges). Îµ-closure is fundamental in the NFA-to-DFA subset construction algorithm.

q: DFA minimization (e.g., using Hopcroft's algorithm) aims to:
options: A) Convert a DFA into an NFA with fewer states
B) Produce the unique smallest DFA (fewest states) that accepts the same language
C) Add dead states to make the DFA complete
D) Convert the DFA back into a regular expression
explanation: DFA minimization produces the minimal DFA â€” the DFA with the fewest states that accepts the same regular language. The algorithm works by partitioning states into equivalence classes: initially {accepting states} and {non-accepting states}, then iteratively splitting groups based on distinguishable transitions. Hopcroft's algorithm does this efficiently in O(n log n) time.

q: In DFA minimization, two states p and q are called 'distinguishable' if:
options: A) They have the same set of outgoing transitions
B) There exists some input string w such that exactly one of Î´*(p,w) and Î´*(q,w) is an accepting state
C) They are both accepting states
D) They are both non-accepting states
explanation: Two states p and q are distinguishable if there exists at least one string w such that starting from p on w leads to an accepting state while starting from q on w does not (or vice versa). Distinguishable states cannot be merged. The minimization algorithm finds all pairs of indistinguishable (equivalent) states and merges them.

q: Which of the following statements about the LEX tool is TRUE?
options: A) LEX generates a parser based on context-free grammar rules
B) LEX takes a specification of token patterns as regular expressions and generates a C program that performs lexical analysis
C) LEX generates code for semantic analysis
D) LEX uses LR parsing internally to recognize tokens
explanation: LEX (Lexical Analyzer Generator) accepts a specification file containing: definitions, rules (regular expression + action pairs), and user code. It generates a C source file (lex.yy.c) implementing a lexical analyzer. Internally, LEX converts the regular expressions to an NFA, then to a DFA, and represents the DFA as a transition table. YACC/Bison, not LEX, handles parsing.

q: A LEX specification file is divided into three sections separated by '%%'. What does the second section contain?
options: A) Global C declarations and macro definitions
B) Rules: regular expression patterns and the C code actions to execute when matched
C) Auxiliary C functions used by the lexer
D) Grammar rules for the parser
explanation: A LEX file has three sections: (1) Definitions â€” global declarations, #include statements, and named regular expression abbreviations; (2) Rules â€” pairs of 'pattern  { action }' where the pattern is a regex and the action is C code to execute on a match; (3) User Code â€” auxiliary C functions. The sections are separated by %% lines.

q: The symbol table in a compiler is primarily used to:
options: A) Store the source code for error recovery
B) Store information about identifiers (name, type, scope, memory location) used throughout compilation
C) Store the sequence of tokens produced by the lexical analyzer
D) Store the parse tree generated by the syntax analyzer
explanation: The symbol table is a data structure maintained throughout compilation. It stores attributes of identifiers: name, data type, scope, dimension (for arrays), number of arguments (for functions), return type, memory address/offset, etc. It is created and populated during lexical/syntax analysis and heavily used during semantic analysis and code generation.

q: Which data structure is most commonly used to implement a symbol table for efficient lookup?
options: D) Binary search tree with O(n) average lookup
explanation: A hash table is the most common implementation for a symbol table because it provides O(1) average-case lookup, insertion, and deletion. The identifier name is hashed to an index. Collisions are resolved via chaining or open addressing. Binary search trees provide O(log n) lookup and are used when ordered traversal is needed.

q: Thompson's construction algorithm is used to:
options: A) Convert a DFA into a minimum-state DFA
B) Convert a regular expression into an equivalent NFA
C) Convert an NFA into an equivalent DFA
D) Convert a context-free grammar into a PDA
explanation: Thompson's construction (1968) is a systematic algorithm that converts any regular expression into an equivalent NFA with Îµ-transitions. It works inductively: base cases handle single characters and Îµ; inductive cases handle concatenation, union (|), and Kleene star (*). The resulting NFA has at most 2n states for a regex of length n.

q: Consider the regular expression a(b|c)*d. Which of the following strings is NOT accepted by this regular expression?
explanation: The regex a(b|c)*d matches strings that start with 'a', are followed by zero or more characters each being 'b' or 'c', and end with 'd'. 'ad' matches (zero repetitions of b|c). 'abcd' and 'abbd' match. 'da' does NOT match because it starts with 'd' and ends with 'a', reversing the required structure.

q: Which of the following regular expressions correctly describes unsigned integers (one or more digits)?
explanation: [0-9]+ means one or more digits (0 through 9), which correctly describes unsigned integers. [0-9]* allows zero digits (empty string), which is not a valid integer. [0-9]? matches zero or one digit. [1-9][0-9] matches exactly a two-digit number not starting with zero. The '+' operator (one or more) is key here.

q: In a DFA, the transition function Î´: Q Ã— Î£ â†’ Q maps:
options: A) A set of states and an input symbol to a set of states
B) A single state and an input symbol to exactly one state
C) A single state and an input symbol to a set of states
D) A set of states and an input symbol to a single state
explanation: In a DFA, Î´: Q Ã— Î£ â†’ Q is the total transition function. For each state q âˆˆ Q and input symbol a âˆˆ Î£, Î´(q, a) gives exactly one (deterministic) next state. In an NFA, the transition function is Î´: Q Ã— (Î£ âˆª {Îµ}) â†’ 2^Q, mapping a state and symbol to a set (possibly empty) of next states.

q: A finite automaton accepts a string w if and only if:
options: A) The automaton reaches its start state after processing w
B) The automaton reaches an accepting (final) state after processing all characters of w
C) Every state visited during processing of w is an accepting state
D) The automaton processes at least one character of w without getting stuck
explanation: A finite automaton (DFA or NFA) accepts string w if, after processing all characters of w starting from the initial state, the automaton is in an accepting (final) state. For an NFA, acceptance means at least one possible computation path ends in an accepting state. Rejection occurs if all paths end in non-accepting states or the automaton gets stuck.

q: Which of the following is TRUE about the relationship between NFA and DFA?
options: A) NFAs can recognize languages that DFAs cannot
B) DFAs can recognize languages that NFAs cannot
C) NFAs and DFAs recognize exactly the same class of languages (regular languages)
D) NFAs are always more efficient than DFAs for string matching
explanation: By the Rabin-Scott theorem, NFAs and DFAs are equivalent in expressive powerâ€”both recognize exactly the class of regular languages. Any NFA can be converted to an equivalent DFA (via subset construction) and vice versa. DFAs are more efficient for simulation (O(n) per input string), while NFAs are more compact to construct from regular expressions.

q: During lexical analysis, which of the following is the correct handling of whitespace in most languages?
options: A) Whitespace is treated as a token and passed to the parser
B) Whitespace (spaces, tabs, newlines) is typically recognized and discarded (not passed as a token)
C) Whitespace causes a lexical error
D) Whitespace is stored in the symbol table
explanation: In most programming languages, whitespace (spaces, tabs, newlines) serves only as a separator between tokens and carries no semantic meaning. The lexical analyzer recognizes whitespace and discards it without generating a token. Some languages like Python treat newlines as significant (statement terminators), but spaces/tabs are still generally discarded except for indentation handling.

q: The 'code optimization' phase of a compiler:
options: A) Guarantees that the generated code runs in minimal possible time on all hardware
B) Improves intermediate code to make the resulting target code faster or smaller, without changing the program's output
C) Converts intermediate code into assembly language
D) Checks the source code for logical errors
explanation: Code optimization transforms intermediate code to improve efficiency (speed, memory, power) without altering the observable behavior (output) of the program. Optimization techniques include: constant folding, common subexpression elimination, dead code elimination, loop invariant code motion, and inlining. It does NOT guarantee absolute optimality â€” it produces 'better' code, not necessarily 'optimal' code.

q: Which of the following best describes the role of the 'code generation' phase?
options: A) It produces a machine-independent intermediate representation
B) It maps the optimized intermediate representation to the target machine's instruction set
C) It checks semantic correctness of the source program
D) It generates the symbol table
explanation: The code generation phase is the final phase of compilation. It translates the (optimized) intermediate representation into the target machine's instruction set (assembly or machine code). It handles register allocation (deciding which values to keep in registers), instruction selection (choosing appropriate machine instructions), and instruction scheduling (ordering instructions to exploit CPU pipelines).

q: In the context of lexical analysis, what is a 'dead state' in a DFA?
options: A) A state from which no string leads to an accepting state (a trap/error state)
B) A state that is also an accepting state
C) The initial state of the DFA
D) A state with no outgoing transitions
explanation: A dead state (also called a trap state or error state) is a non-accepting state in a DFA from which all transitions lead back to itself â€” once the DFA enters this state, it can never reach an accepting state. Dead states are added to make a DFA 'complete' (total transition function). In lexical analyzers, entering a dead state signals that no token pattern matches the current input.

q: The 'front end' of a compiler consists of which phases?
options: A) Code Optimization and Code Generation
B) Lexical Analysis, Syntax Analysis, Semantic Analysis, and Intermediate Code Generation
C) Only Lexical Analysis and Syntax Analysis
D) Intermediate Code Generation, Code Optimization, and Code Generation
explanation: A compiler is conceptually divided into a front end and a back end. The front end handles source-language-dependent analysis: Lexical Analysis, Syntax Analysis, Semantic Analysis, and Intermediate Code Generation. The back end handles target-machine-dependent synthesis: Code Optimization and Code Generation. This separation enables retargeting (same front end, different back ends for different architectures).

q: Which of the following correctly identifies a valid identifier token in C, given the pattern [a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_]*?
explanation: The C identifier pattern [a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_]* requires the first character to be a letter (a-z, A-Z) or underscore (_), followed by zero or more letters, digits, or underscores. '_myVar123' satisfies this: starts with '_', followed by valid characters. '2variable' and '123abc' start with digits (invalid). 'my-var' contains a hyphen (invalid in identifiers).

q: In NFA-to-DFA conversion using the subset construction, the DFA's start state is:
options: A) The set of all NFA states
B) The Îµ-closure of the NFA's start state
C) The NFA's start state alone (as a singleton set)
D) The set of all accepting states of the NFA
explanation: In the subset construction algorithm, the DFA's start state is the Îµ-closure of the NFA's initial state q0. This is because from q0, the NFA can spontaneously (via Îµ-transitions) reach other states without consuming input. The Îµ-closure captures all states reachable from q0 before reading any input character.

q: A DFA state in the subset construction is an accepting state if and only if:
options: A) It corresponds to a subset containing only accepting NFA states
B) It corresponds to a subset containing at least one accepting NFA state
C) It is the state corresponding to the entire set of NFA states
D) It corresponds to a subset that does not contain the NFA start state
explanation: In the subset construction, a DFA state (which represents a subset S of NFA states) is an accepting state if and only if S contains at least one accepting (final) state from the original NFA. This ensures that any computation path of the NFA that ends in an accepting state is also accepted by the DFA.

q: What is the maximum number of states a DFA can have when constructed from an NFA with n states using the subset construction?
explanation: Using the subset construction, each DFA state corresponds to a subset of the NFA's n states. Since there are 2^n possible subsets of an n-state NFA, the resulting DFA can have at most 2^n states. In practice, many subsets may not be reachable and the DFA often has far fewer states, but the worst-case exponential blowup is achievable (e.g., for the language of strings where the n-th from last character is 'a').

q: Regular expressions are used in lexical analysis because they:
options: A) Can describe all possible programming language constructs including context-sensitive ones
B) Provide a concise, formal notation for specifying token patterns, and can be mechanically converted to finite automata
C) Are more powerful than context-free grammars
D) Can describe the structure of nested parentheses in expressions
explanation: Regular expressions are the ideal formalism for specifying token patterns because: (1) they are concise and readable; (2) they have a well-defined mathematical foundation; (3) they can be mechanically converted to NFAs (via Thompson's construction) and then to DFAs for efficient simulation. However, regular expressions cannot describe nested/recursive structures (like balanced parentheses) â€” those require context-free grammars.

q: Which of the following is an example of a 'keyword' token in the C language?
explanation: Keywords are reserved words in a programming language with fixed meaning â€” they cannot be used as identifiers. In C, 'while' is a keyword (reserved for the while loop). 'main' and 'printf' are identifiers (function names) â€” they have special conventional meaning but are not reserved keywords in C. 'myFunction' is a user-defined identifier.

q: The syntax analysis (parsing) phase of a compiler uses which formalism to describe the structure of programs?
explanation: Syntax analysis uses context-free grammars (CFGs) to describe the hierarchical structure of programming language constructs. CFGs are more powerful than regular expressions and can describe nested structures (like balanced parentheses, nested if-else). The parser takes the token stream from the lexer and builds a parse tree (or AST) according to the grammar. Regular expressions (and finite automata) are insufficient for parsing.

q: Which of the following is a valid operation in regular expression algebra? (where L and M are regular languages)
options: A) Complement of a regular language is not regular
B) The intersection of two regular languages is always regular
C) Regular languages are not closed under concatenation
D) The Kleene star of a regular language may not be regular
explanation: Regular languages are closed under all of: union, concatenation, Kleene star, complement, intersection, and difference. Therefore, the intersection of two regular languages is always regular. Options A, C, and D are all FALSE â€” regular languages ARE closed under complement, concatenation, and Kleene star.

q: In a LEX specification, when multiple rules match the current input, LEX resolves the conflict by:
options: A) Reporting an error and stopping
B) Choosing the rule that matches the longest string; if tied, choosing the rule listed first
C) Choosing the rule listed last in the specification
D) Choosing the rule that matches the shortest string
explanation: LEX uses two disambiguation rules: (1) Maximal munch (longest match) â€” the lexer always takes the longest possible match. For example, '>=' is matched as a single token rather than '>' followed by '='. (2) Rule priority â€” if two patterns match the same (longest) string, the rule that appears first in the LEX specification takes priority. This is why keywords are listed before the general identifier pattern.

q: What does the 'error handling' component of a lexical analyzer do when it encounters an invalid character?
options: B) Skips the invalid character or invokes an error routine, then attempts to continue scanning
C) Treats the invalid character as whitespace
D) Passes the invalid character to the parser as a special error token that the parser must handle
explanation: When a lexical analyzer encounters a character that does not match any token pattern (lexical error), it typically: reports the error with line/column information, then either skips the offending character (panic mode recovery) or replaces it with a valid one, and continues scanning. Some implementations pass a special ERROR token to the parser. Immediate termination is poor practice as it prevents finding multiple errors.

q: Which of the following is NOT a phase of a compiler but rather a 'pass'?
options: B) Reading through the entire source/intermediate code once
explanation: A 'phase' is a logical stage of compilation (lexical analysis, syntax analysis, etc.). A 'pass' refers to a single traversal over the entire source code or intermediate representation. A compiler may be single-pass (does all phases in one traversal) or multi-pass (makes multiple passes). 'Reading through the entire code once' describes a pass, not a phase. Most modern compilers use multiple passes for better optimization.

q: The process of 'input buffering' in a lexical analyzer is used to:
options: A) Store the symbol table entries during scanning
B) Efficiently read the source code from disk using two buffers, allowing lookahead without repeated disk I/O
C) Buffer the token stream before sending it to the parser
D) Maintain a stack of scoping levels for the symbol table
explanation: Lexical analyzers use input buffering (typically a two-buffer scheme) to efficiently read source code. Since lexers need lookahead (reading ahead to determine token boundaries, e.g., '>=' vs '>'), doing individual character I/O calls is expensive. The two-buffer scheme reads large blocks from disk at a time. A 'forward' pointer scans ahead while a 'beginning' pointer marks the start of the current lexeme.

q: Which of the following statements about the symbol table is CORRECT?
options: A) The symbol table is constructed only during semantic analysis
B) The symbol table is used only in the code generation phase
C) The symbol table is populated during lexical and syntax analysis and consulted throughout all subsequent phases
D) A separate symbol table is maintained for each function and they are never merged
explanation: The symbol table is a central data structure used throughout compilation. It begins to be populated during lexical analysis (identifiers are entered) and syntax analysis (type/scope info added). It is heavily used in semantic analysis (type checking, scope resolution), intermediate code generation, optimization, and code generation (for memory layout and addressing). Scoped symbol tables (one per scope) are common but linked together.

q: Consider an NFA with 3 states. Using the subset construction, the equivalent DFA has at most how many states?
explanation: For an NFA with n = 3 states, the subset construction can produce at most 2^n = 2^3 = 8 DFA states (one for each subset of the 3 NFA states: âˆ…, {0}, {1}, {2}, {0,1}, {0,2}, {1,2}, {0,1,2}). In practice, many subsets may be unreachable, resulting in fewer states, but the theoretical maximum is 2^3 = 8.

q: A Context-Free Grammar (CFG) is defined by which 4-tuple?
options: (V, T, P, S) where V=variables, T=terminals, P=productions, S=start symbol
(V, T, P, E) where E=empty string
(N, Î£, Î´, q0) where Î´=transition function
(Q, Î£, Î“, Î´) where Î“=stack alphabet
explanation: A CFG is formally defined as a 4-tuple (V, T, P, S): V = set of non-terminals (variables), T = set of terminals, P = set of production rules, S = start symbol (S âˆˆ V).

q: In a Context-Free Grammar, which of the following is TRUE about production rules?
options: Left-hand side must be a single non-terminal
Left-hand side can be any string of terminals and non-terminals
Right-hand side must contain at least one terminal
Left-hand side can be a string of non-terminals only
explanation: In a CFG, every production rule has the form A â†’ Î±, where A is a single non-terminal and Î± is any string of terminals and/or non-terminals (including Îµ). This distinguishes CFGs from context-sensitive grammars.

q: Which of the following strings can be derived from the grammar S â†’ aSb | Îµ?
explanation: The grammar S â†’ aSb | Îµ generates strings of the form aâ¿bâ¿ (n â‰¥ 0). Starting with S: S â†’ aSb â†’ aaSbb â†’ aaÎµbb = aabb. Option B 'ab' is also derivable (Sâ†’aSbâ†’aÎµb=ab), but 'aabb' is the most illustrative correct choice among the options.

q: In a leftmost derivation, at each step we replace:
options: The leftmost non-terminal in the sentential form
The rightmost non-terminal in the sentential form
Any non-terminal in the sentential form
explanation: In a leftmost derivation (denoted â‡’lm), at each step the leftmost non-terminal in the current sentential form is replaced by the right-hand side of one of its productions. This is the convention used in top-down parsing.

q: In a rightmost derivation, at each step we replace:
options: The rightmost non-terminal in the sentential form
The leftmost non-terminal in the sentential form
explanation: In a rightmost derivation (denoted â‡’rm), at each step the rightmost non-terminal in the current sentential form is replaced. Rightmost derivations correspond to bottom-up parsing (shift-reduce parsing).

q: A parse tree for a string w with grammar G has which property?
options: Root is the start symbol, interior nodes are non-terminals, leaves are terminals or Îµ
Root is a terminal, leaves are non-terminals
explanation: A parse tree (derivation tree) has: root labeled with start symbol S, each interior node labeled with a non-terminal A, children of A correspond to the RHS of some production A â†’ X1X2...Xk, and leaves are terminals or Îµ.

q: The yield (frontier) of a parse tree is:
options: The string formed by reading the leaves from left to right
The set of all non-terminals in the tree
explanation: The yield (or frontier) of a parse tree is obtained by reading all the leaf nodes from left to right, which gives the derived terminal string (or sentential form).

q: A grammar is said to be ambiguous if:
options: There exists a string with more than one leftmost derivation (or parse tree)
It has more than one start symbol
explanation: A grammar G is ambiguous if there exists at least one string w âˆˆ L(G) that has two or more distinct parse trees (equivalently, two distinct leftmost derivations or two distinct rightmost derivations).

q: The classic example of an ambiguous grammar is:
options: E â†’ E+T | T, T â†’ T*F | F
explanation: E â†’ E+E | E*E | id is the classic ambiguous grammar. The string id+id*id has two parse trees depending on whether + or * binds first. The unambiguous version uses precedence: Eâ†’E+T|T, Tâ†’T*F|F.

q: Which of the following is a property of an inherently ambiguous language?
options: Every CFG generating it is ambiguous
It can be generated by some unambiguous CFG
explanation: A context-free language is inherently ambiguous if every CFG that generates it is ambiguous. There is no way to find an equivalent unambiguous grammar. An example is {aâ±bÊ²cáµ | i=j or j=k}.

q: Top-down parsing begins from:
options: The start symbol and tries to derive the input string
The input string and tries to reduce to start symbol
The leaves of the parse tree
explanation: Top-down parsing starts at the root (start symbol) and attempts to construct the parse tree downward, matching the input string. It corresponds to leftmost derivation. Examples: recursive descent parsing, LL(1) parsing.

q: Recursive descent parsing uses:
options: A set of mutually recursive procedures, one for each non-terminal
explanation: In recursive descent parsing, each non-terminal in the grammar has a corresponding recursive procedure. The procedures call each other to match the input. It implements top-down, leftmost derivation.

q: A predictive parser is a top-down parser that:
options: Never needs to backtrack by using lookahead symbols
Requires the grammar to be right-recursive
explanation: A predictive parser (e.g., LL(1) parser) uses k lookahead symbols (k=1 for LL(1)) to determine the correct production to apply without backtracking. The grammar must be unambiguous, non-left-recursive, and left-factored.

q: The first 'L' in LL(1) stands for:
explanation: In LL(1): the first 'L' = Left-to-right scanning of the input, the second 'L' = Leftmost derivation is produced, and '1' = one lookahead symbol is used at each step to make parsing decisions.

q: The second 'L' in LL(1) stands for:
explanation: In LL(1), the second 'L' stands for Leftmost derivation â€” the parser constructs a leftmost derivation of the input. The first L = left-to-right scanning, and 1 = one lookahead symbol.

q: FIRST(Î±) for a string Î± is defined as:
options: The set of terminals that begin strings derivable from Î±
The set of non-terminals that begin strings derivable from Î±
The set of all symbols that can follow Î±
The set of terminals that can follow A
explanation: FIRST(Î±) is the set of terminals a such that Î± â‡’* aÎ² for some Î². If Î± â‡’* Îµ, then Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(Î±). It contains all terminals that can appear as the first symbol of any string derived from Î±.

q: FOLLOW(A) for a non-terminal A is defined as:
options: The set of terminals that can appear immediately to the right of A in some sentential form
The set of terminals that begin strings derivable from A
The set of non-terminals that follow A in all productions
The set of all symbols in A's productions
explanation: FOLLOW(A) = {a âˆˆ T | S â‡’* Î±AaÎ² for some Î±, Î²}. It is the set of terminals (and $ for end-of-input) that can appear immediately after A in any sentential form derivable from S.

q: $ (end-marker) is always in FOLLOW of:
explanation: By convention, $ (end-of-input marker) is always placed in FOLLOW(S) where S is the start symbol. This is the base case for computing FOLLOW sets.

q: For grammar S â†’ AB, A â†’ a | Îµ, B â†’ b, what is FIRST(S)?
explanation: FIRST(S) = FIRST(AB). FIRST(A) = {a, Îµ}. Since Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(A), we also include FIRST(B) = {b}. So FIRST(S) = {a, b}. Îµ is not included since B cannot derive Îµ.

q: For grammar S â†’ aA | b, A â†’ c | Îµ, what is FOLLOW(A)?
explanation: A appears in S â†’ aA. Nothing follows A in this production, so we include FOLLOW(S) = {$}. Therefore FOLLOW(A) = {$}.

q: The LL(1) parsing table entry M[A, a] is filled with production A â†’ Î± when:
options: a âˆˆ FIRST(Î±), or (Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(Î±) and a âˆˆ FOLLOW(A))
A â†’ Î± is the first production for A
explanation: M[A,a] = Aâ†’Î± if: (1) a âˆˆ FIRST(Î±) â€” terminal a can start a derivation from Î±, OR (2) Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(Î±) AND a âˆˆ FOLLOW(A) â€” Î± can derive Îµ and a follows A in context.

q: A grammar is LL(1) if and only if its LL(1) parsing table has:
options: No multiply-defined entries (at most one production per entry)
At least one entry per row
No left recursion and multiple entries allowed
explanation: A grammar is LL(1) if and only if its parsing table M[A,a] has at most one production for every non-terminal A and terminal a (no conflicts). Multiple entries indicate ambiguity or left recursion.

q: Left recursion in a grammar rule A â†’ AÎ± | Î² causes problems for top-down parsers because:
options: The parser enters infinite recursion without consuming input
The parser cannot build a parse tree
explanation: With A â†’ AÎ± | Î², a top-down parser attempting to expand A would again expand A, leading to infinite recursion with no input consumed. LL(1) parsers cannot handle left-recursive grammars.

q: The standard technique to eliminate immediate left recursion A â†’ AÎ± | Î² is to replace it with:
options: A â†’ Î²A', A' â†’ Î±A' | Îµ
A â†’ A'Î², A' â†’ Î±A'
explanation: Immediate left recursion A â†’ AÎ± | Î² is eliminated by introducing a new non-terminal A': A â†’ Î²A', A' â†’ Î±A' | Îµ. This produces the same language using right recursion, which is compatible with top-down parsing.

q: After eliminating left recursion from E â†’ E+T | T, the result is:
options: E â†’ TE', E' â†’ +TE' | Îµ
E â†’ E'T, E' â†’ +T | Îµ
explanation: Applying left recursion elimination: Î² = T, Î± = +T. Result: E â†’ TE', E' â†’ +TE' | Îµ. This is the standard arithmetic expression grammar used in most compiler textbooks.

q: Left factoring is applied when a grammar has productions like A â†’ Î±Î² | Î±Î³. The left-factored form is:
options: A â†’ Î±A', A' â†’ Î² | Î³
explanation: Left factoring: factor out the common prefix Î±. Result: A â†’ Î±A', A' â†’ Î² | Î³. This eliminates the problem of the parser not knowing which alternative to choose when it sees Î±, making LL(1) parsing possible.

q: Left factoring is necessary because:
options: LL(1) parser cannot decide between alternatives sharing a common prefix with one lookahead
explanation: If A â†’ Î±Î² | Î±Î³, seeing terminal a âˆˆ FIRST(Î±) doesn't tell the parser which production to use. Left factoring defers the decision: A â†’ Î±A', A' â†’ Î² | Î³, allowing one lookahead to resolve the choice after consuming Î±.

q: Consider grammar: S â†’ if E then S | if E then S else S. This grammar is:
explanation: This is the classic dangling else ambiguity. The string 'if E then if E then S else S' has two parse trees â€” 'else' can be matched with either 'if'. This is a well-known example of an ambiguous grammar.

q: For grammar A â†’ aAb | ab, which string is NOT in the language?
explanation: A â†’ aAb | ab generates strings of the form aâ¿bâ¿ (n â‰¥ 1). Valid strings: ab(n=1), aabb(n=2), aaabbb(n=3). 'aaabb' = aÂ²bÂ² would need n=2 giving aabb, not aaabb. So aaabb (3 a's, 2 b's) is NOT in the language.

q: In top-down parsing using a stack, when we pop non-terminal A and the lookahead is a, we:
options: Look up M[A,a] and push the RHS of the production onto the stack (right to left)
explanation: In table-driven LL(1) parsing: if top of stack is non-terminal A and lookahead is a, we look up M[A,a]. If M[A,a] = Aâ†’X1X2...Xk, we pop A and push Xk...X2X1 (right to left so X1 is on top).

q: In top-down parsing, if the top of the stack is terminal a and the current input symbol is also a, we:
options: Pop a from the stack and advance the input pointer
Push a duplicate onto the stack
explanation: When top-of-stack matches the current input terminal, we perform a match: pop the terminal from the stack and advance the input pointer to the next symbol. This is the 'match' action in LL(1) parsing.

q: FIRST(Îµ) is:
explanation: FIRST(Îµ) = {Îµ}. By definition, FIRST(Î±) includes Îµ if Î± can derive the empty string. Since Îµ directly IS the empty string, FIRST(Îµ) = {Îµ}.

q: For production A â†’ Îµ in LL(1) table construction, we add A â†’ Îµ to M[A, b] for every b in:
explanation: For A â†’ Îµ: since Îµ âˆˆ FIRST(Îµ), we add Aâ†’Îµ to M[A,b] for all b âˆˆ FOLLOW(A). This handles cases where A should disappear (derive Îµ) when the lookahead is a symbol that follows A.

q: Which of the following grammars is NOT LL(1)?
options: S â†’ aB | b, B â†’ c
explanation: S â†’ aS | a: FIRST(aS) = {a} and FIRST(a) = {a}. Both alternatives start with 'a', causing a conflict in M[S,a]. This grammar is NOT LL(1). The others have disjoint FIRST sets for alternatives.

q: To compute FIRST(A) for a non-terminal A with production A â†’ X1X2...Xk:
options: Add FIRST(X1)\{Îµ}; if ÎµâˆˆFIRST(X1), add FIRST(X2)\{Îµ}, and so on; add Îµ only if all Xi can derive Îµ
Add all terminals in the production
explanation: For A â†’ X1X2...Xk: add FIRST(X1)-{Îµ}. If ÎµâˆˆFIRST(X1), also add FIRST(X2)-{Îµ}. Continue until either Xi cannot derive Îµ or all Xi can derive Îµ (in which case add Îµ to FIRST(A)).

q: For grammar S â†’ AB, A â†’ a | Îµ, B â†’ b | Îµ, what is FIRST(S)?
explanation: FIRST(S)=FIRST(AB). FIRST(A)={a,Îµ}. Since ÎµâˆˆFIRST(A), include FIRST(B)={b,Îµ}. Since ÎµâˆˆFIRST(B) too and no more symbols, include Îµ. So FIRST(S)={a,b,Îµ}.

q: In computing FOLLOW sets, the rule for S â†’ Î±BÎ² (where B is a non-terminal) is:
options: Add FIRST(Î²)\{Îµ} to FOLLOW(B); if ÎµâˆˆFIRST(Î²), add FOLLOW(S) to FOLLOW(B)
Add FIRST(Î²) including Îµ to FOLLOW(B)
explanation: For S â†’ Î±BÎ²: everything in FIRST(Î²)-{Îµ} can follow B, so add it to FOLLOW(B). If ÎµâˆˆFIRST(Î²) (Î² can be empty), then everything following S can also follow B, so add FOLLOW(S) to FOLLOW(B).

q: The grammar E â†’ E+E | E*E | (E) | id has how many parse trees for id+id*id?
explanation: For id+id*id with Eâ†’E+E|E*E|id: (1) (id+id)*id â€” + binds first, then *; (2) id+(id*id) â€” * binds first, then +. These give 2 distinct parse trees, confirming the grammar is ambiguous.

q: Which condition makes a grammar unsuitable for LL(1) parsing?
explanation: Left recursion (direct: Aâ†’AÎ±, or indirect) makes a grammar unsuitable for LL(1)/top-down parsing as it causes infinite loops. Right recursion is handled fine. Îµ-productions are allowed in LL(1) grammars.

q: The general algorithm to eliminate all (direct and indirect) left recursion requires first:
options: Ordering non-terminals A1,...,An and eliminating indirect left recursion by substitution
explanation: The general left recursion elimination algorithm: (1) Order non-terminals A1,...,An. (2) For i=1 to n: substitute Aj (j<i) productions into Aiâ†’Aj... to expose direct left recursion. (3) Eliminate direct left recursion in Ai.

q: A sentential form is:
options: Any string of terminals and non-terminals derivable from the start symbol
Only strings of terminals derivable from start symbol
The right-hand side of any production
explanation: A sentential form is any string Î± (of terminals and/or non-terminals) such that S â‡’* Î±. A sentence is a sentential form consisting entirely of terminals. The language L(G) is the set of all sentences.

q: In an LL(1) parsing table, an ERROR entry M[A,a] means:
options: No production exists for non-terminal A when lookahead is a â€” syntax error
A â†’ Îµ should be applied
explanation: A blank/error entry M[A,a] means there is no valid production for non-terminal A when the current input symbol is a. The parser should report a syntax error and invoke error recovery.

q: The ACCEPT action in LL(1) parsing occurs when:
options: The stack contains only $ and the input is also $
explanation: In LL(1) table-driven parsing, the ACCEPT action occurs when the top of the stack is $ (end marker) AND the current input symbol is also $. This means the entire input has been successfully parsed.

q: Consider grammar: A â†’ Î±Î² | Î±Î³ | Î´. After left factoring, the correct form is:
options: A â†’ Î±A' | Î´, A' â†’ Î² | Î³
A â†’ Î± | Î´, A â†’ Î² | Î³
A â†’ Î´A', A' â†’ Î±
explanation: Left factoring extracts common prefix Î± from Aâ†’Î±Î² and Aâ†’Î±Î³: Aâ†’Î±A'|Î´, A'â†’Î²|Î³. The production Aâ†’Î´ has no common prefix with others so it remains as is.

q: For grammar S â†’ iEtSS' | a, S' â†’ eS | Îµ, E â†’ b (solution to dangling else), what is the type of this grammar?
options: LL(1) â€” unambiguous version of dangling else
explanation: Sâ†’iEtSS'|a with S'â†’eS|Îµ is the standard unambiguous resolution of the dangling else: each else is associated with the nearest unmatched if. This grammar is LL(1) and resolves the ambiguity.

q: In a parse tree, the number of interior nodes equals:
options: The number of production rule applications used in the derivation
The number of terminals in the string
The number of non-terminals in the grammar
The length of the input string
explanation: Each interior node in a parse tree represents one application of a production rule (one derivation step). If we used n production rule applications to derive a string, the parse tree has exactly n interior nodes.

q: Which of the following grammars generates the language {aâ¿bâ¿ | n â‰¥ 0}?
explanation: Sâ†’aSb|Îµ: when n=0, Sâ‡’Îµ; when n=1, Sâ‡’aSbâ‡’aÎµb=ab; when n=2, Sâ‡’aSbâ‡’aaSbbâ‡’aaÎµbb=aabb. This generates exactly {aâ¿bâ¿|nâ‰¥0}. Option D generates {aâ¿bâ¿|nâ‰¥1}.

q: A grammar G is ambiguous. Which statement is TRUE?
options: L(G) may still be an unambiguous language (another grammar might be unambiguous for L(G))
L(G) must be an inherently ambiguous language
G cannot be used for parsing
explanation: An ambiguous grammar doesn't mean the language is inherently ambiguous. For example, Eâ†’E+E|E*E|id is ambiguous, but the same language can be generated by the unambiguous grammar Eâ†’E+T|T, Tâ†’T*F|F, Fâ†’id.

q: The time complexity of LL(1) parsing for an input of length n is:
explanation: LL(1) parsing runs in O(n) time where n is the length of the input. Each input symbol is processed (matched) exactly once, and each production application takes O(1) time using the precomputed parsing table.

q: For grammar E â†’ TE', E' â†’ +TE' | Îµ, T â†’ FT', T' â†’ *FT' | Îµ, F â†’ (E) | id, what is FIRST(E')?
explanation: FIRST(E') = FIRST(+TE') âˆª FIRST(Îµ) = {+} âˆª {Îµ} = {+, Îµ}. The production E'â†’+TE' starts with terminal '+', and E'â†’Îµ contributes Îµ to FIRST(E').

q: In shift-reduce parsing, which of the following actions can be performed by the parser?
explanation: A shift-reduce parser can perform four actions: shift (push next input symbol onto stack), reduce (replace handle on stack with non-terminal), accept, or error.

q: A 'handle' in bottom-up parsing is defined as:
options: The leftmost non-terminal in a sentential form
A substring that matches the right-hand side of a production and whose reduction leads to a valid parse
The topmost symbol on the parse stack
The first symbol of the input string
explanation: A handle is a substring Î± of a right-sentential form Î³ such that A â†’ Î± is a production and reducing Î± to A is the right step in a rightmost derivation in reverse.

q: Which of the following correctly describes a 'viable prefix' in LR parsing?
options: Any prefix of the input string
A prefix of a right-sentential form that does not extend past the right end of a handle
A prefix that contains only terminals
explanation: A viable prefix is a prefix of a right-sentential form that can appear on the stack of a shift-reduce parser; it does not extend beyond the right end of the handle.

q: LR(0) items are used to construct which type of automaton?
explanation: LR(0) items are used to build a DFA (finite automaton) that recognizes viable prefixes of the grammar, forming the canonical LR(0) collection.

q: An LR(0) item is a production with:
options: A dot placed somewhere in the right-hand side
The left-hand side replaced by a non-terminal
Two dots indicating start and end
explanation: An LR(0) item is a production rule with a dot (â€¢) placed at some position in the right-hand side, indicating how much of the production has been seen.

q: The CLOSURE operation on a set of LR(0) items adds items for productions of the form:
options: A â†’ Î± â€¢ when â€¢ is at the end
B â†’ â€¢Î³ for every production B â†’ Î³ where â€¢ is immediately to the left of B in some item
All items with the same left-hand side
Items where the dot is at the beginning
explanation: If A â†’ Î±â€¢BÎ² is in the set, then for every production B â†’ Î³, we add B â†’ â€¢Î³ to the set. This is the CLOSURE operation.

q: The GOTO function in LR parsing construction takes:
options: A state and a production rule
A set of items and a grammar symbol, returning the closure of items where the dot is moved past that symbol
Two states and returns a transition
A non-terminal and returns a set of terminals
explanation: GOTO(I, X) = CLOSURE({A â†’ Î±Xâ€¢Î² | A â†’ Î±â€¢XÎ² âˆˆ I}). It computes the set of items reachable from item set I on symbol X.

q: In SLR(1) parsing, reduce actions are added for item A â†’ Î±â€¢ in state i when:
options: The input symbol is any terminal
The input symbol is in FOLLOW(A)
The input symbol is in FIRST(A)
The next state exists for that terminal
explanation: In SLR(1), if A â†’ Î±â€¢ is in state i, we add 'reduce by A â†’ Î±' for all terminals a in FOLLOW(A). This is the key distinction of SLR(1) over LR(0).

q: LALR(1) parsing differs from SLR(1) in that:
options: It uses larger lookahead sets computed from the actual LR(1) items rather than FOLLOW sets
It does not handle reduce-reduce conflicts
It works only on LL grammars
explanation: LALR(1) merges LR(1) states with the same core (LR(0) part) and uses the precise lookahead sets from LR(1) items, making it more powerful than SLR(1) but with the same number of states.

q: Which parsing method is the most powerful among the following?
explanation: CLR(1) (Canonical LR(1)) is the most powerful as it uses the full LR(1) items with precise lookaheads, capable of parsing all deterministic context-free languages. The power order is: LR(0) âŠ‚ SLR(1) âŠ‚ LALR(1) âŠ‚ CLR(1).

q: An LR(1) item has the form [A â†’ Î±â€¢Î², a] where 'a' represents:
options: A lookahead terminal used to decide when to reduce
The next state in the automaton
explanation: In an LR(1) item [A â†’ Î±â€¢Î², a], 'a' is the lookahead terminal. When Î² = Îµ, the parser reduces by A â†’ Î± only if the current input is 'a'.

q: A shift-reduce conflict in LR parsing occurs when:
options: Two different reduce actions are possible in a state
Both a shift action and a reduce action are possible in the same state for the same input symbol
The stack is empty and input is not exhausted
explanation: A shift-reduce conflict arises when the parser cannot decide between shifting the next input symbol or reducing the current handle for the same lookahead symbol.

q: A reduce-reduce conflict in LR parsing occurs when:
options: Two productions can be used to shift in the same state
In a given state, the same lookahead symbol triggers reduction by two different productions
The input string has two possible leftmost derivations
Two shift actions are possible for the same symbol
explanation: A reduce-reduce conflict occurs when a single state has items for two different complete productions A â†’ Î±â€¢ and B â†’ Î²â€¢, both with the same lookahead symbol.

q: YACC (Yet Another Compiler Compiler) generates which type of parser by default?
explanation: YACC generates an LALR(1) parser by default, as LALR(1) provides a good balance between parsing power and table size (same as LR(0) number of states but more precise).

q: In YACC, a grammar rule is written in the format:
explanation: In YACC, grammar rules are written as 'left_side : right_side { semantic_action }' where the colon separates the left and right sides, and actions are in curly braces.

q: Operator precedence parsing is based on which type of grammar?
options: Operator grammars (no production has two adjacent non-terminals or empty right side)
explanation: Operator precedence parsing works with operator grammars, which have the property that no production right-hand side is empty or has two adjacent non-terminals.

q: The three precedence relations used in operator precedence parsing are:
options: â‹–, â‰, â‹— (yields precedence, equal precedence, takes precedence)
explanation: The three operator precedence relations are: a â‹– b (a yields precedence to b, meaning b binds tighter), a â‰ b (a and b have equal precedence), and a â‹— b (a takes precedence over b).

q: In operator precedence parsing, reduction happens when the top of stack operator has relation ___ with the current input operator.
explanation: When the top-of-stack operator 'takes precedence' (â‹—) over the current input operator, the parser reduces. This corresponds to the top of stack being the right end of a handle.

q: The canonical LR(1) collection is obtained by:
options: Applying CLOSURE and GOTO to LR(0) items only
Applying CLOSURE and GOTO starting from the augmented grammar's initial LR(1) item
Merging all LR(1) states with the same core
Computing FOLLOW sets for all non-terminals
explanation: The canonical LR(1) collection is built by computing CLOSURE and GOTO starting from the initial item [S' â†’ â€¢S, $] of the augmented grammar, producing all LR(1) item sets.

q: LALR(1) is obtained from CLR(1) by:
options: Merging states that have the same LR(0) core (same items ignoring lookaheads)
explanation: LALR(1) is constructed by merging LR(1) states that have the same core (same set of LR(0) items), combining their lookahead sets. This reduces the number of states while retaining most of CLR(1)'s power.

q: Which of the following statements about LALR(1) vs CLR(1) is TRUE?
options: LALR(1) has more states than CLR(1)
LALR(1) can have reduce-reduce conflicts that CLR(1) does not
CLR(1) can have conflicts that LALR(1) resolves
LALR(1) and CLR(1) always accept the same languages
explanation: Merging states in LALR(1) can introduce reduce-reduce conflicts that don't exist in CLR(1), because different lookaheads from merged states might conflict. LALR(1) never introduces new shift-reduce conflicts though.

q: The augmented grammar for G with start symbol S introduces a new production:
explanation: To augment grammar G with start symbol S, we add a new start symbol S' and the production S' â†’ S. This ensures the initial item has a unique accept state.

q: In an LR parsing table, the ACTION table entries can be:
explanation: The ACTION table entries are: s_n (shift to state n), r_k (reduce by production k), acc (accept), or blank/error. The GOTO table only contains state numbers for non-terminals.

q: For an LR(0) parser to have no conflicts, every state must satisfy:
options: At most one reduce item and no shift items
Either all shift items or exactly one reduce item with no shift items
All items must be complete (dot at end)
explanation: For LR(0), a state can either contain only shift items, or exactly one reduce item (and no shift items). If it has both a complete item and shift items, or two complete items, there's a conflict.

q: The handle of a right-sentential form in bottom-up parsing is always found:
options: At the beginning of the string
At the top of the parse stack (leftmost in remaining portion)
At the end of the input
explanation: In shift-reduce parsing, the handle always appears at the top of the stack. The parser works by finding and reducing the handle iteratively until the start symbol is obtained.

q: Which of the following grammars CANNOT be parsed by an operator precedence parser?
options: All of the above can cause issues
explanation: Operator grammars cannot have empty (Îµ) right-hand sides. A grammar with Îµ-productions violates the operator grammar condition, making operator precedence parsing inapplicable.

q: In YACC, the %left, %right, and %nonassoc declarations are used to:
options: Specify operator associativity and resolve shift-reduce conflicts
explanation: In YACC, %left, %right, and %nonassoc declare operator associativity. They also implicitly define precedence levels (listed tokens have increasing precedence) to resolve shift-reduce conflicts.

q: The number of states in an LALR(1) parser compared to a CLR(1) parser for the same grammar is:
explanation: LALR(1) is constructed by merging CLR(1) states with the same core, so it always has fewer than or equal to the number of states in CLR(1). It's never more.

q: The precedence function f and g in operator precedence parsing satisfy:
options: f(a) > g(b) when a â‹— b
f(a) < g(b) when a â‹– b, f(a) = g(b) when a â‰ b, f(a) > g(b) when a â‹— b
f(a) and g(b) are always different
explanation: The precedence functions f and g encode precedence relations: f(a) < g(b) for a â‹– b, f(a) = g(b) for a â‰ b, and f(a) > g(b) for a â‹— b. Not all precedence tables can be encoded as functions.

q: In LR parsing, when the parser performs a 'reduce' action using production A â†’ Î² (|Î²| = n), it:
options: Pops n symbols from the stack and pushes A, then transitions based on GOTO
Pushes n symbols onto the stack
Moves the input pointer back by n positions
explanation: On a reduce by A â†’ Î² where |Î²| = n, the parser pops 2n items (n state-symbol pairs) from the stack, then pushes A and uses GOTO[top_state, A] to find the new state.

q: Which of the following is NOT a valid LR(0) item for production A â†’ BCD?
explanation: An LR(0) item has exactly one dot (â€¢) placed at one position in the production right-hand side. A â†’ Bâ€¢Câ€¢D has two dots, which is not a valid LR item.

q: The 'viable prefix' property ensures that:
options: The parser never makes an error on a prefix that is part of a valid complete input
The stack never grows beyond a certain size
explanation: Viable prefixes are exactly those prefixes of right-sentential forms that can appear on the LR parser stack. This guarantees the parser only accumulates stack contents consistent with valid parse prefixes.

q: In SLR(1) parsing table construction, the GOTO table is filled based on:
options: Transitions on non-terminals from the LR(0) automaton states
explanation: GOTO[i, A] = j means that from state i, on non-terminal A, the parser transitions to state j. This is determined by GOTO(I_i, A) in the LR(0) automaton construction.

q: YACC input file has three sections separated by %%: declarations, rules, and:
explanation: A YACC input file has three sections separated by %%: (1) declarations (token definitions, %start, %left, etc.), (2) grammar rules with actions, and (3) user code (auxiliary C functions like main() and yyerror()).

q: Which conflict type is generally considered more serious in LR parsing?
explanation: Reduce-reduce conflicts are generally considered more serious because they indicate fundamental ambiguity in the grammar â€” the parser cannot determine which production to use, often signaling a grammar design flaw.

q: For the grammar E â†’ E+E | E*E | id, a shift-reduce conflict arises because:
options: The grammar is ambiguous â€” multiple parse trees exist for the same string
explanation: E â†’ E+E | E*E | id is ambiguous. For input like id+id*id, the parser cannot decide whether to reduce E+E first or shift * (i.e., which operator has higher precedence), causing a shift-reduce conflict.

q: The canonical LR(1) item [A â†’ Î±â€¢BÎ², a] generates which closure items?
options: [B â†’ â€¢Î³, a] for all B â†’ Î³
[B â†’ â€¢Î³, b] for all B â†’ Î³ and b âˆˆ FIRST(Î²a)
[B â†’ â€¢Î³, b] for all B â†’ Î³ and b âˆˆ FOLLOW(B)
explanation: The closure of [A â†’ Î±â€¢BÎ², a] adds [B â†’ â€¢Î³, b] for every production B â†’ Î³ and every terminal b âˆˆ FIRST(Î²a). This is because 'b' is what could follow B in this context.

q: In operator precedence parsing, the 'prime phrase' (simple phrase) is:
options: The longest phrase with no inner prime phrases
The shortest phrase of a sentential form with no inner phrases â€” it is the handle
Any non-terminal in the parse tree
explanation: In operator precedence parsing, a prime phrase (simple phrase) is a phrase that contains no smaller phrase. It corresponds to the handle and is the candidate for reduction.

q: LR parsing is called 'bottom-up' because:
options: The parse tree is built from the root down
The parse tree is constructed from leaves to root by reducing handles
The input is processed from right to left
The stack grows from bottom to top
explanation: LR parsing builds the parse tree from leaves (terminals) upward to the root (start symbol) by repeatedly identifying and reducing handles, reversing a rightmost derivation.

q: The number of LR(1) items for a grammar is generally _____ the number of LR(0) items.
explanation: Each LR(0) item A â†’ Î±â€¢Î² can generate multiple LR(1) items [A â†’ Î±â€¢Î², a] for different lookaheads 'a'. So the number of LR(1) items is always â‰¥ the number of LR(0) items.

q: In YACC, the default rule for resolving shift-reduce conflicts is:
explanation: YACC's default conflict resolution for shift-reduce conflicts is to prefer shift over reduce. For reduce-reduce conflicts, it prefers the production listed first in the grammar.

q: The SLR(1) parsing method is called 'Simple' LR because:
options: It uses a simpler automaton than LR(0)
It uses the simple approximation of FOLLOW sets instead of precise per-item lookaheads
It has fewer states than LR(0)
It parses a smaller class of grammars
explanation: SLR(1) is called 'Simple' because it uses the FOLLOW set (a global approximation) to determine lookaheads for reduce actions, rather than computing precise per-item lookaheads like CLR(1)/LALR(1).

q: For a grammar to be an operator grammar (suitable for operator precedence parsing), it must satisfy:
options: No left recursion and no right recursion
No production has an empty right-hand side and no two adjacent non-terminals in any right-hand side
All productions must be in Chomsky Normal Form
All non-terminals must be reachable from the start symbol
explanation: An operator grammar requires: (1) no Îµ-productions, and (2) no production right-hand side has two consecutive non-terminals. These ensure that every pair of adjacent operators has a defined precedence relation.

q: The 'kernel' of an LR(0) item set consists of:
options: All items with the dot at the beginning
All items except those of the form A â†’ â€¢Î± (initial items), plus the initial item S' â†’ â€¢S
explanation: The kernel of an item set is the set of items with the dot not at the leftmost position, plus the special initial item S' â†’ â€¢S. Non-kernel items are those with the dot at the start (added by closure).

q: Which statement about LR(0) vs SLR(1) is correct?
options: Every LR(0) grammar is also SLR(1)
Every SLR(1) grammar is also LR(0)
LR(0) and SLR(1) accept exactly the same grammars
Every SLR(1) grammar is parseable by LR(0) without conflicts if FOLLOW sets are restricted
explanation: Every LR(0) grammar is also SLR(1) because SLR(1) is a strict superset of LR(0). SLR(1) adds lookahead constraints (FOLLOW sets) that can resolve conflicts present in LR(0).

q: In constructing the LR(0) canonical collection, the initial item set Iâ‚€ is:
options: CLOSURE({S â†’ â€¢Î±}) for all productions of S
CLOSURE({S' â†’ â€¢S}) where S' â†’ S is the augmented production
All items with dot at position 0
explanation: Iâ‚€ is computed as CLOSURE({S' â†’ â€¢S}) where S' â†’ S is the new production added when augmenting the grammar. This is the starting state of the LR automaton.

q: A grammar G is said to be LALR(1) but NOT SLR(1) if:
options: Its LR(1) states have reduce-reduce conflicts
SLR(1) has conflicts that LALR(1) resolves using more precise lookaheads
LALR(1) has more states than SLR(1)
SLR(1) cannot handle the grammar's left recursion
explanation: Some grammars have SLR(1) conflicts because FOLLOW sets are too approximate, but LALR(1) resolves them using precise per-state lookaheads. Such grammars are LALR(1) but not SLR(1).

q: In operator precedence parsing, if a â‰ b (equal precedence), it typically indicates:
options: A syntax error in the grammar
The operators associate (same precedence level, e.g., a+a or matching parentheses)
explanation: The â‰ relation indicates equal precedence and is used for operators at the same precedence level (same associativity group) or for matching delimiters like ( and ), allowing the handle body to be identified.

q: The total number of possible LR(0) items for a production A â†’ BCDE is:
explanation: For a production with n symbols on the right-hand side, there are n+1 LR(0) items (dot can be in n+1 positions). For A â†’ BCDE (4 symbols), there are 5 items: Aâ†’â€¢BCDE, Aâ†’Bâ€¢CDE, Aâ†’BCâ€¢DE, Aâ†’BCDâ€¢E, Aâ†’BCDEâ€¢.

q: YACC handles the lexical analysis part through:
options: A separate lexer, typically generated by LEX, which YACC calls via yylex()
explanation: YACC relies on an external lexical analyzer (typically generated by LEX/Flex) called via the yylex() function. YACC handles parsing while LEX handles tokenization â€” they work as a complementary pair.

q: Which of the following best describes the relationship between the four LR parsers in terms of grammar classes they can parse?
options: LR(0) âŠ‚ SLR(1) âŠ‚ LALR(1) âŠ‚ CLR(1)
CLR(1) âŠ‚ LALR(1) âŠ‚ SLR(1) âŠ‚ LR(0)
LR(0) âŠƒ SLR(1) âŠƒ LALR(1) âŠƒ CLR(1)
explanation: The class of grammars parseable increases strictly: every LR(0) grammar is SLR(1), every SLR(1) grammar is LALR(1), and every LALR(1) grammar is CLR(1). CLR(1) can parse all deterministic CFLs.

q: What is a Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD)?
options: A context-free grammar with semantic actions attached to each production
A grammar that defines the syntax of a language only
A method to parse tokens from a lexer
A type of regular expression for semantic rules
explanation: An SDD is a context-free grammar where each production rule has associated semantic rules (actions) that compute attribute values.

q: In a Syntax-Directed Definition, a synthesized attribute of a non-terminal A is computed from:
options: Attributes of A's parent in the parse tree
Attributes of A's children and A itself in the parse tree
Only the terminal symbols in the grammar
The inherited attributes of sibling nodes
explanation: Synthesized attributes are computed bottom-up: their values depend on the attributes of children nodes (and possibly the node itself) in the parse tree.

q: An inherited attribute of a non-terminal B in a production A â†’ Î±BÎ² is computed from:
options: Attributes of A (parent) and siblings Î±, Î²
Only terminal symbols on the right-hand side
The synthesized attributes of B's children
explanation: Inherited attributes are passed top-down: they are computed using the parent's and siblings' attributes in the parse tree.

q: Which of the following grammars uses ONLY synthesized attributes?
explanation: An S-attributed grammar is a special case of SDD where all attributes are synthesized â€” no inherited attributes are used.

q: An L-attributed grammar allows inherited attributes that depend on:
options: Any attribute anywhere in the parse tree
Only attributes of the parent and left siblings in the same production
explanation: In L-attributed grammars, inherited attributes of a symbol on the RHS can only depend on the parent's inherited attributes and attributes of symbols to its LEFT in the same production.

q: Which of the following is TRUE about S-attributed grammars?
options: They can be evaluated during bottom-up parsing
They cannot be used with LR parsers
explanation: S-attributed grammars use only synthesized attributes, which are computed bottom-up, making them perfectly compatible with bottom-up (LR) parsers.

q: L-attributed grammars are suitable for evaluation during:
options: Both LR and LL parsing equally
explanation: L-attributed grammars can be evaluated in a single left-to-right pass, making them suitable for top-down (LL) parsing.

q: Every S-attributed grammar is also:
explanation: Every S-attributed grammar is a special case of L-attributed grammar because synthesized attributes trivially satisfy the L-attributed constraints.

q: An annotated parse tree is a parse tree in which:
options: Each node carries the values of its attributes
Only the root node has an attribute value
explanation: An annotated parse tree shows the parse tree with computed attribute values attached to each node, illustrating how semantic rules are evaluated.

q: In a syntax-directed translation scheme (SDTS), semantic actions are:
options: Placed only at the end of each production
Embedded within the right-hand side of productions
explanation: In an SDTS, semantic actions are program fragments embedded at any position within the right-hand side of a grammar production.

q: Which attribute is computed during a bottom-up traversal of a parse tree?
explanation: Synthesized attributes are computed bottom-up â€” a parent's synthesized attribute is computed after all children's attributes are computed.

q: Which of the following is an example of a synthesized attribute?
options: The type information passed from a declaration to the variable in its scope
The value of an arithmetic expression computed from its sub-expressions
The environment passed down through nested scopes
The offset of a variable passed from parent to children
explanation: The value of an arithmetic expression (e.g., E.val) is synthesized from the values of sub-expressions (children), making it a classic synthesized attribute.

q: Which of the following is a classic example of an inherited attribute?
options: The value of an integer literal
The length of a string expression
The type passed from a declaration to identifiers in a variable list
The result of a function call
explanation: In a declaration like 'int a, b, c;', the type 'int' is an inherited attribute passed from the declaration non-terminal down to each identifier in the list.

q: Static type checking is performed:
options: At runtime by the operating system
At compile time by the compiler
explanation: Static type checking occurs at compile time. The compiler verifies type correctness without executing the program.

q: Dynamic type checking is performed:
explanation: Dynamic type checking is done at runtime. The program checks type compatibility when the relevant code is actually executed.

q: Which of the following languages primarily uses static type checking?
explanation: Java uses static type checking â€” types are declared and verified at compile time. Python, Ruby, and JavaScript are dynamically typed.

q: A type expression is used to describe:
options: The runtime value of a variable
The structure and nature of a data type
The memory address of a variable
The control flow of a program
explanation: A type expression describes the type of a language construct â€” it can be a basic type (int, float) or a composite type (array, pointer, function).

q: Which of the following is NOT a basic type expression in most type systems?
explanation: Array is a type constructor (composite type), not a basic type. Basic types include integer, boolean, real, char, etc.

q: Structural type equivalence means two types are equivalent if:
options: They have the same structure (same fields in the same order)
They are declared in the same scope
They are assigned to the same variable
explanation: Structural equivalence checks if two types have identical structure â€” same components, same order, same types of components â€” regardless of their names.

q: Name type equivalence means two types are equivalent if:
options: They are the same declared type (same name or same declaration)
They occupy the same memory size
They have the same number of fields
explanation: Name equivalence holds two types as equivalent only if they refer to the same type declaration (same name or literally the same declaration in some implementations).

q: In C language, struct types use which form of type equivalence?
options: Neither; C has no type equivalence rules
explanation: C uses name equivalence for struct types â€” two separately defined structs with identical fields are NOT considered the same type.

q: Type coercion refers to:
options: Explicit conversion of a type by the programmer
Implicit automatic conversion of a type by the compiler
Removing type information from a value
Declaring a variable with multiple types
explanation: Type coercion is an implicit (automatic) type conversion performed by the compiler, e.g., converting int to float in a mixed arithmetic expression.

q: Type casting refers to:
options: Implicit type conversion done by the compiler
Explicit type conversion requested by the programmer
Assigning a default type to untyped variables
explanation: Type casting is an explicit conversion written by the programmer, e.g., (float)x in C, to convert a value from one type to another.

q: Which of the following is an example of implicit type coercion?
options: float y = 5; // int 5 promoted to float automatically
explanation: Assigning an integer literal 5 to a float variable without any explicit cast is implicit coercion â€” the compiler automatically promotes int to float.

q: Static scoping (lexical scoping) means:
options: The scope of a variable is determined at runtime based on the call stack
The scope of a variable is determined at compile time based on the program text
Variables are accessible from any function
Scope is determined by the order of function execution
explanation: In static (lexical) scoping, the scope of a variable is determined by its position in the source code â€” the compiler resolves names based on the textual structure.

q: Dynamic scoping means:
options: Scope is determined by the textual structure of the program
Scope is determined at runtime based on the calling sequence of functions
explanation: In dynamic scoping, the scope of a variable is determined at runtime by the call chain â€” a function can access variables of the calling function.

q: Which of the following languages uses static scoping?
explanation: C uses static (lexical) scoping. The scope of every variable is determined by its position in the source code.

q: In a type system, the 'width' of a basic type refers to:
options: The number of characters in the type name
The number of bytes needed to store a value of that type
The range of values the type can hold
The number of operations defined on the type
explanation: Width of a type is the number of storage units (bytes) needed to represent a value of that type, used in computing offsets for record/struct fields.

q: In syntax-directed definitions, the dependency graph is used to:
options: Determine a valid order to evaluate attributes
Draw the parse tree of a string
Define the semantic actions for each rule
explanation: A dependency graph represents the dependencies between attribute instances. An evaluation order is valid if it follows a topological sort of this graph.

q: A circular dependency in an attribute grammar means:
options: There is no valid evaluation order for the attributes
explanation: If the dependency graph has a cycle, there is no valid topological order to evaluate attributes â€” the attribute grammar is said to be circular and unusable.

q: Which of the following is TRUE about an S-attributed SDD evaluated on an LR parse tree?
options: Attributes require a separate post-order traversal
Only inherited attributes can be computed
The parser stack cannot hold attribute values
explanation: For S-attributed SDDs, synthesized attributes are computed during reductions in LR parsing â€” each reduce action triggers evaluation of semantic rules.

q: A type system is considered 'sound' if:
options: It accepts all well-typed programs and rejects all type-unsafe programs
It never produces type errors at runtime for well-typed programs
It allows implicit coercion between all types
explanation: A sound type system guarantees that well-typed programs will never encounter type errors at runtime â€” safety is proven at compile time.

q: The type expression for a function that takes an integer and returns a float is written as:
